framework.reasoning: This paper contains no trained model -- it is a suite of Monte-Carlo simulations, deterministic calibrated formulas, and on-chain data reconciliation estimates (SIR training_pipeline confidence 0.92 that this is genuinely N/A). PyTorch is selected only to satisfy the SIR schema's required 'primary framework' field; the actual computational stack is NumPy (Monte-Carlo sampling, vectorised scenario sweeps) and pandas (tabular reconciliation of the Bitcoin/Ethereum exposure data sources and the top-20 readiness table). No CUDA/GPU is needed -- every model here is a scalar or low-dimensional Monte-Carlo simulation (thousands of draws over 3-4 parameters) or a closed-form calculation, trivially fast on CPU. YAML config covers all swept simulation ranges, calibration constants, and the many `# ASSUMED` values needed to fill gaps the paper itself leaves narrative rather than formulaic (see SIR ambiguities).

module_hierarchy.description: Bottom-up physics/hardware-scaling estimator: models physical-qubit count growth, the falling cryptanalytic resource requirement, and a fault-tolerance readiness lag, to produce a naive-then-corrected CRQC feasibility year distribution

public_methods.name: sample_break_years
public_methods.signature: sample_break_years(self, n_draws: int, rng: np.random.Generator) -> np.ndarray
public_methods.returns: array of shape [n_draws] of sampled CRQC feasibility years, incorporating the hardware-doubling, resource-halving, and fault-tolerance-lag draws

public_methods.name: crossing_year
public_methods.signature: crossing_year(self, doubling_time: float, halving_time: float, q0: float, r0: float, t0: float) -> float
public_methods.returns: the naive year at which physical-qubit count Q(t) first exceeds resource requirement R(t)

module_hierarchy.description: Expert-survey-based CRQC arrival estimator (Global Risk Institute / Mosca-Piani style), modeled as a parametric distribution calibrated to the paper's reported mode (~2038-2040) and spread

public_methods.name: sample_break_years
public_methods.signature: sample_break_years(self, n_draws: int, rng: np.random.Generator) -> np.ndarray
public_methods.returns: array of shape [n_draws] of sampled CRQC arrival years drawn from a distribution calibrated to the expert-survey mode/spread

module_hierarchy.description: Blends the physics-based and survey-based estimators into the paper's headline bimodal systemic break-year forecast (Figure 2), and reports cumulative probabilities and credible ranges

public_methods.name: run
public_methods.signature: run(self, n_draws: int = 100000, survey_weight: float = 0.5, seed: int = 0) -> Dict

public_methods.name: sensitivity_sweep
public_methods.signature: sensitivity_sweep(self, weights: List[float], n_draws: int = 100000, seed: int = 0) -> pd.DataFrame
public_methods.returns: DataFrame of cdf_2035/median per survey_weight, reproducing the paper's 0.25-0.75 weight-sensitivity statement

module_hierarchy.description: Grover-vs-classical-proof-of-work mining model: effective quantum hashrate vs gate speed, sqrt(K) parallelization wall, comparison against one ASIC and the full network (Figure 1)

public_methods.name: effective_hashrate
public_methods.returns: effective single-machine hashrate in TH/s, linearly scaled from the Aggarwal et al. 2017 calibration benchmark (13.8 GH/s @ 66.7 MHz)

public_methods.name: parallel_hashrate
public_methods.signature: parallel_hashrate(self, gate_speed_ghz: float, n_machines: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray
public_methods.returns: effective network hashrate for K quantum machines, scaling as sqrt(K) (the Grover parallelization wall)

public_methods.name: machines_for_51_percent
public_methods.signature: machines_for_51_percent(self, gate_speed_ghz: float, network_hashrate_ehs: float) -> float
public_methods.returns: number of quantum machines required to reach 51% of the given network hashrate under sqrt(K) scaling

module_hierarchy.description: Reconciles independent Bitcoin on-chain exposure measurements (Glassnode, Coinbase, CoinDesk, Deloitte) into the paper's irreducible/migratable/protected supply decomposition (Figure 3)

public_methods.name: reconcile_sources
public_methods.returns: a single reconciled exposed-supply fraction from multiple named source measurements (ASSUMED: simple mean of sources, see config.yaml)

public_methods.name: decompose
public_methods.signature: decompose(self, total_supply_btc: float, exposed_fraction: float, irreducible_btc: float, migratable_btc: float) -> Dict[str, float]
public_methods.returns: dict with irreducible_btc, migratable_btc, protected_btc, and their fractions of total_supply_btc, matching Figure 3

module_hierarchy.description: Cross-checks Ethereum at-rest exposure via a top-down composition (total supply minus staked minus contract holdings) and a bottom-up on-chain component build, reconciling to the paper's 50-65% (most defensibly 55-60%) range

public_methods.name: top_down_estimate
public_methods.signature: top_down_estimate(self, staked_fraction: float, contract_fraction: float) -> float

public_methods.name: bottom_up_estimate
public_methods.signature: bottom_up_estimate(self, naive_beacon_overcount_fraction: float, correction_factor: float) -> float
public_methods.returns: corrected on-chain exposed-account fraction (ASSUMED correction method, see config.yaml)

public_methods.name: reconcile
public_methods.signature: reconcile(self, top_down: float, bottom_up: float) -> Tuple[float, float]

module_hierarchy.description: Models the fast-clock-CRQC mempool-sniping race: deriving a private key from a spent public key within the block-confirmation window and broadcasting a fee-bump replacement before the original transaction confirms

public_methods.name: snipe_success_probability
public_methods.signature: snipe_success_probability(self, derivation_minutes: float, propagation_delay_minutes: float, confirmations_required: int, block_interval_minutes: float = 10.0) -> float
public_methods.returns: probability the attacker's replacement transaction confirms before the original -- ASSUMED race-condition formula (ratio of remaining block-interval time to total race time), calibrated to reproduce the paper's stated 41% best-case figure

public_methods.name: is_feasible
public_methods.returns: False for slow-clock machines (trapped-ion, neutral-atom) which cannot derive a key inside the confirmation window; True for fast-clock machines (superconducting, photonic)

module_hierarchy.description: Implements Mosca's inequality as a migration-race decision rule across start-time/adoption scenarios, comparing migration-finish year against CRQC arrival estimates (Figure 4)

public_methods.name: is_at_risk
public_methods.signature: is_at_risk(self, start_year: float, migration_duration_years: float, crqc_arrival_year: float) -> bool

public_methods.name: run_scenarios
public_methods.signature: run_scenarios(self, scenarios: Dict[str, Dict], crqc_estimates: Dict[str, float]) -> pd.DataFrame
public_methods.returns: DataFrame of migration-finish-year per named start-time scenario (prompt/delayed/severe-delay), each compared against the named CRQC arrival estimates (aggressive/survey-median/conservative), matching Figure 4

module_hierarchy.description: Top-20 cryptocurrency quantum-readiness qualitative rating (1-5 ordinal scale), loaded from a config-driven table rather than computed, since the paper itself describes this as 'a sourced field assessment, not a model' (Table 3, Figure 5)

public_methods.name: load_ratings
public_methods.returns: DataFrame with columns coin, signature_scheme, exposure_model, pq_status, rating -- transcribed directly from the paper's Table 3, not independently re-derived

public_methods.name: summary_stats
public_methods.returns: dict with mean/median rating, count at each rating level, and the 'none reaches 5' check

module_hierarchy.description: YAML config loading and global random-seed management
Reproduces Figure 1 (hashrate vs gate speed), Figure 2 (bimodal break-year forecast), Figure 3 (Bitcoin exposure pie), Figure 4 (migration-race bar chart), Figure 5 (top-20 readiness bar chart)

functions.name: plot_figure1_hashrate
functions.signature: plot_figure1_hashrate(gate_speeds: np.ndarray, hashrates: np.ndarray, asic_hashrate: float, network_hashrate: float, save_path: str = None) -> plt.Figure

functions.name: plot_figure2_forecast
functions.signature: plot_figure2_forecast(survey_samples: np.ndarray, physics_samples: np.ndarray, combined_samples: np.ndarray, save_path: str = None) -> plt.Figure
functions.returns: matplotlib figure matching Figure 2 (bimodal histogram overlay)

functions.name: plot_figure3_exposure_pie
functions.signature: plot_figure3_exposure_pie(decomposition: Dict[str, float], save_path: str = None) -> plt.Figure

functions.name: plot_figure4_migration_race
functions.signature: plot_figure4_migration_race(scenario_results: pd.DataFrame, crqc_lines: Dict[str, float], save_path: str = None) -> plt.Figure

functions.name: plot_figure5_readiness
functions.signature: plot_figure5_readiness(ratings_df: pd.DataFrame, save_path: str = None) -> plt.Figure
functions.returns: matplotlib horizontal bar chart matching Figure 5

tensor_flows.name: Systemic break-year Monte-Carlo forecast pipeline

input.name: swept parameter ranges (hardware doubling time, resource-requirement halving time, fault-tolerance lag, survey weight)

output.name: combined break-year sample array + cumulative probabilities at 2035/2040/2050 + 80% range
output.shape: [n_draws] samples -> 3 scalar CDF values + 2 scalar range bounds

steps: PhysicsBasedEstimator.sample_break_years() draws n_draws physics-model break years using randomly sampled doubling_time ~ U(1.0,2.5), halving_time ~ U(4,20), fault_tolerance_lag ~ U(2,12) (ASSUMED uniform distributions, config.yaml documents this)

SurveyBasedEstimator.sample_break_years() draws n_draws survey-model break years from a distribution calibrated to mode~2038-2040 (ASSUMED parametric form, e.g. a skewed/lognormal distribution, config.yaml documents this)
SystemicForecastModel.run() combines the two sample arrays via random selection with probability survey_weight per draw (ASSUMED sample-level mixture, not density-level -- see SIR ambiguities)
compute empirical CDF at 2035/2040/2050 and the 10th/90th percentile (80% range) from the combined sample array
plot_figure2_forecast() renders the bimodal histogram overlay matching Figure 2

tensor_flows.name: Bitcoin and Ethereum exposure reconciliation pipeline

input.name: named on-chain source measurements (Glassnode, Coinbase, CoinDesk, Deloitte for Bitcoin; staked fraction, contract fraction, Beacon-deposit overcount for Ethereum)

output.name: Bitcoin: [irreducible, migratable, protected] decomposition; Ethereum: [consensus_range_low, consensus_range_high]

steps: BitcoinExposureModel.reconcile_sources() averages the named source measurements (ASSUMED simple mean, config.yaml documents this)

BitcoinExposureModel.decompose() splits the reconciled exposed fraction into irreducible/migratable/protected using the paper's stated headline BTC counts (2.3M/3.7M) as fixed inputs rather than re-derived quantities
EthereumExposureModel.top_down_estimate() and .bottom_up_estimate() compute the two cross-checking fractions
EthereumExposureModel.reconcile() combines them into the paper's 50-65% (most defensibly 55-60%) consensus range
plot_figure3_exposure_pie() renders the Bitcoin decomposition matching Figure 3

tensor_flows.name: Migration-race decision pipeline

input.name: start-time scenarios (prompt 2026 / delayed 2030 / severe-delay 2033), migration-duration assumptions, CRQC arrival estimates

output.name: migration-finish year per scenario + at-risk boolean per (scenario, CRQC-estimate) pair

steps: MigrationRaceModel.run_scenarios() computes migration-finish year for each start-time scenario using the paper's reported adoption-scenario durations (ASSUMED reconstruction from the reported finish years, config.yaml documents this)
MigrationRaceModel.is_at_risk() checks Mosca's inequality for each (scenario, CRQC-estimate) pair
plot_figure4_migration_race() renders the bar chart matching Figure 4

model.monte_carlo_n_draws: 100000  # not specified in paper; chosen for a stable empirical CDF
model.monte_carlo_distribution_form: uniform  # ASSUMED: paper gives ranges but not distributional shape (SIR confidence 0.45, ambiguity #1)
model.survey_estimator_distribution_form: lognormal  # ASSUMED: chosen to be right-skewed matching the paper's described survey-hump shape (SIR confidence 0.45)

bitcoin.reconciliation_method: simple_mean  # ASSUMED: paper does not give exact reconciliation formula (SIR ambiguity #3)

ethereum.contract_fraction: 0.08  # ASSUMED: not given numerically; chosen so top_down = 1-0.32-0.08 = 0.60, within the paper's stated 55-63% top-down range (SIR ambiguity #3)
ethereum.beacon_overcount_correction_factor: 2.3  # ASSUMED: chosen so bottom_up lands near the paper's stated 45-55% range (SIR ambiguity #3)

mempool_race.realistic_propagation_delay_minutes: 2.0  # ASSUMED: not given numerically; calibrated so realistic_success_probability lands near the paper's stated 30% (SIR ambiguity #2)

runtime.reason: distributional forms for the survey estimator (lognormal), statistical utilities
tabular reconciliation of exposure sources, migration-scenario sweeps, and the top-20 readiness table

entrypoints.purpose: Convenience wrapper running all five analyses in sequence

risk_assessment.description: The Monte-Carlo forecast's exact distributional forms (for the swept doubling/halving/lag ranges and for the survey estimator) and the precise mixture method (sample-level vs density-level) are not specified in the paper. Different reasonable choices could shift the reported by-2035/by-2040/by-2050 probabilities noticeably, since the paper itself notes the by-2035 figure is sensitive to the survey weight alone (8-24% across 0.25-0.75).
risk_assessment.mitigation: config.yaml exposes every distributional-form choice explicitly with '# ASSUMED' comments; SystemicForecastModel.sensitivity_sweep() reproduces the paper's own stated 0.25-0.75 weight-sensitivity check so a user can see how much of the headline result is sensitive to this modeling choice, rather than treating the reproduced numbers as more precise than the paper itself claims them to be.

risk_assessment.description: The mempool-race model's internal timing/queueing formula connecting derivation time, propagation delay, and confirmation count to a success probability is not given; the 'realistic 30%' figure is backed into via an ASSUMED propagation-delay parameter tuned to hit that target, not independently derived.
risk_assessment.mitigation: MempoolRaceModel.snipe_success_probability() takes propagation_delay_minutes as an explicit, documented parameter (config.yaml) rather than hard-coding the 30% output, so a user can see exactly which input was tuned and substitute their own timing model if they have access to the paper's actual code (github.com/imgcode/quantum-horizon, cited but not available to this pipeline).

risk_assessment.description: Both exposure-reconciliation models' source-combination method (simple mean vs weighted average vs source-quality judgment) is not given; the specific 2.3M/3.7M Bitcoin split and the Ethereum contract_fraction/beacon_overcount_correction_factor values are back-calculated to match the paper's headline numbers rather than independently derived from the raw source measurements.
risk_assessment.mitigation: BitcoinExposureModel.reconcile_sources() and EthereumExposureModel's methods take the raw source measurements as inputs and the reconciliation method as a named, swappable strategy (config.yaml 'reconciliation_method' field), so a user can substitute weighted-average or other reconciliation logic without touching the decomposition/cross-check logic downstream.

risk_assessment.description: The top-20 quantum-readiness survey (Section 6) is explicitly analyst judgment, not a computed model (the paper itself says so in Appendix A). Table 3's data must be manually transcribed into data/table3_readiness_ratings.csv rather than derived from any formula, and could drift from the paper if transcribed incorrectly.
risk_assessment.source: SIR architecture.modules 'Top-20 cryptocurrency quantum-readiness survey', confidence 0.5
risk_assessment.mitigation: QuantumReadinessSurvey.load_ratings() reads from a versioned CSV transcribed directly from the paper's Table 3, with a unit test asserting the transcribed ratings match the paper's reported values (e.g. 'no top-20 coin reaches 5', Bitcoin and Dogecoin near the bottom) to catch transcription drift.

risk_assessment.description: The physics-based estimator's initial calibration constants (Q_0, R_0, t_0) are back-solved from the paper's stated 2026 boundary conditions rather than given directly, which could introduce compounding error if the boundary-condition values (2026 hardware/requirement state) are themselves ranges rather than point estimates.
risk_assessment.mitigation: config.yaml exposes physics_calibration_2026_physical_qubits and the requirement bounds as explicit named fields, and PhysicsBasedEstimator.crossing_year() is unit-tested to confirm it reproduces a plausible 2040s-2050s naive crossing year before the fault-tolerance lag is added, matching the paper's stated physics-estimator mode of ~2052.
